title: Network Growth Simulator Guide
summary: Understand current demand, monthly growth, projection length, peak factor, overhead, target utilization, and uplink size before an upgrade is needed.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool projects bandwidth demand over time and estimates when the modeled load may exceed the target utilization policy for a selected uplink size.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it for expansion planning, budget timing, WAN or uplink upgrades, branch growth, camera growth, and any network design expected to carry more traffic later.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Current Demand (Mbps)
items.description: The current or starting traffic demand before monthly growth is applied.
items.whyItMatters: This is the baseline that all future demand is projected from.
items.commonMistake: Starting from a calm average when the upgrade question depends on busy-period traffic.

links.label: Estimate current demand from devices
links.note: Use this when the starting demand needs to be built from endpoint counts.

items.label: Monthly Growth (%)
items.description: The expected month-over-month increase in demand.
examples: growth from new users or cameras
items.whyItMatters: Small monthly growth rates compound over time and can shorten the usable life of an uplink.
items.commonMistake: Treating growth as a one-time bump instead of a repeating trend.

items.label: Months to Project
items.description: The number of months included in the growth forecast.
items.whyItMatters: The projection window determines whether the tool is checking near-term fit or longer-term upgrade timing.
items.commonMistake: Using too short a window for infrastructure that is hard to replace later.

items.label: Peak Factor
items.description: A multiplier applied to projected demand to represent busy periods, bursts, or worst-case operating bands.
items.whyItMatters: Peak factor keeps the future model from being based only on smooth average growth.
items.commonMistake: Projecting average demand and then being surprised by peak congestion.

links.label: Understand peak factor in base bandwidth planning
links.note: Use this when the peak multiplier should match the original bandwidth estimate.

items.label: Overhead (%)
items.description: A percentage allowance for protocol overhead, management traffic, retransmits, and other non-payload load.
items.whyItMatters: Overhead reduces the useful portion of the uplink as demand grows.
items.commonMistake: Assuming all projected Mbps are clean application payload.

items.label: Target Utilization (%)
items.description: The maximum utilization you want the uplink to reach before upgrade pressure is flagged.
items.whyItMatters: This converts raw link size into a practical planning threshold.
items.commonMistake: Waiting until a link hits 100% before planning the next capacity step.

links.label: Compare target against layer oversubscription
links.note: Use this when the same utilization policy applies across access or aggregation.

items.label: Uplink Size (Gbps)
items.description: The nominal uplink size being tested against projected demand.
items.whyItMatters: The selected link size determines when projected demand crosses the operating threshold.
items.commonMistake: Choosing an upgrade size without checking how long it will last.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the selected uplink should remain inside the target utilization through the projection. Watch means the design approaches the limit during the planning window. Risk means the uplink may need upgrade before the end of the projection.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
